video_title: The Oil Bull Market Is Just Getting Started – Jeff Currie
gist: Jeff Currie (Abax Markets, ex-Goldman) argues that 3 months after a US-Iran war disrupted ~12 mb/d of Gulf supply, oil inventories are draining 5-6 mb/d toward a July 'day zero', the freshly announced US-Iran peace deal is unsustainable and already priced in, and oil equities back at January levels are a uniquely attractive buy on an intact 'revenge of the old economy' thesis. He pairs deep skepticism of 30-40x tech/AI multiples with a contrarian call to rotate capital aggressively into molecules, metals and copper for cash-yielding diversification.

insights.claim: US oil inventories are draining at 5-6 million barrels per day, with the SPR at a 43-year low and product inventories below the 5-year safety band, a draw rate Currie calls 'unheard of'.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: An inventory sinkhole at this pace forces a near-term policy or price response; watch weekly EIA inventory and SPR draw data as the key tape.
insights.quote: the draw rates are still somewhere in that 6 million barrels a day range, 5 to 6 million barrels per day. That's just unheard of.

insights.claim: Currie expects the US to hit 'day zero' on the SPR sometime in mid-to-late July, as the strategic reserve falls from ~340 million barrels toward a 270-300 million critical minimum while drawing 7-10 million barrels a week.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A July deadline is a hard catalyst window; positioning ahead of peak Q3 demand season could front-run the market pricing it in.
insights.quote: That means 40 to 50 million barrels from now. Um and we're we're averaging somewhere between 7 and 10 a week.

insights.claim: Currie clarifies that 'day zero' is not running tanks to empty but hitting minimum operating pressure in the system, a level he says oil markets have breached repeatedly (1996, 2000, 2007-08, 2012-13, 2022) and which historically triggers price explosions.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Reframes the trigger from a tail risk to a recurring, observable mechanism; tank-bottom episodes are a repeatable trade setup, not a one-off.
insights.quote: hitting day zero has nothing to do with running to very tank bottoms. It's just like the system it needs pressure in it.

insights.claim: July 4th matters because third quarter is global peak oil demand, combining US peak driving season with agricultural harvest demand, which is when low inventories bite hardest.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Seasonal demand peak stacked on depleted inventories sets the maximum-stress window for price; calendar the risk into Q3.
insights.quote: third quarter is the global peak demand for for oil

insights.claim: Currie views the announced US-Iran memorandum of understanding as structurally unsustainable, noting the GCC countries were not consulted, Israel is not on board, and the signing date keeps slipping (last week, then Sunday, now Friday).
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A deal that can collapse on a Hezbollah or Israel action means the bearish oil move on peace news is reversible; fade the relief rally.
insights.quote: There's nothing sustainable about it, but the market's trying to price it in now. Almost we've almost erased everything.

insights.claim: Even with a signed deal, oil supply may not return quickly because insurers and ship operators may refuse to send vessels back through the strait, with Currie citing that Maersk said it is not changing its plans.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Shipping and insurance reluctance, not the deal itself, governs the supply timeline; monitor tanker insurance and carrier announcements as the real signal.
insights.quote: if I were a insurance provider or a ship operator, I don't know if I'd bring a ship back in there. I think Maersk just said came out they're not changing their plans.

insights.claim: Currie says the market has already retraced most of the war premium, with oil futures near 80 versus a ~70-72 pre-war level, even though fundamentals are far worse than three months ago.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A price that has unwound the war premium while supply is structurally tighter is a fundamentals-vs-price dislocation to exploit on the long side.
insights.quote: Fundamentally, this market is way worse off today than what it was 3 months ago. Yet the price is, you know, 10 bucks off the levels it was before it started

insights.claim: The Iranian deal terms include roughly $300 billion in reparations to be paid by GCC neighbors, a $24 billion package (with $12 billion up front), plus a logistics servicing fee for use of the strait, which Currie flags as large and hard to fund.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Onerous and contested financial terms add a layer of deal-breakage risk that keeps a geopolitical floor under oil.
insights.quote: they're going to get the 12 billion up front of the 24. Um, plus the 300 billion. Those are big numbers.

insights.claim: The supply disruption spans all Gulf producers (Iran, Kuwait, Iraq, Bahrain, Saudi, UAE), with Saudi and UAE able to restart in roughly three weeks but Kuwait and Iraq at the far end of the Gulf potentially taking months to a year.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A staggered, multi-month restart means supply returns slower than headlines imply; the laggard producers define how long the squeeze lasts.
insights.quote: With the other ones it could be months if not you know take a year.

insights.claim: Currie warns that shutting in older Gulf fields risks permanent damage as reservoir pressure drops and water cuts rise, citing Iran's 1979 revolution where 6 million bpd was shut in and only 4 million ever came back, and a possible 20% permanent loss.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Permanent reserve impairment, not just delay, would structurally raise the global cost curve; a bullish tail the market is ignoring.
insights.quote: it took a while they shut in 6 million barrels per day and ended up only getting four back

insights.claim: Currie uses COVID as the precedent for slow recovery, noting US production took two to three years to climb back to its pre-COVID 13 mb/d peak, which is why oil topped $100 even before the Russia-Ukraine invasion.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: History says restart timelines are measured in years, not weeks, supporting a durable rather than transient price impact.
insights.quote: You look at US production it took two to three years to recover.

themes.theme: Asia exposure and China's electron state

insights.claim: Currie argues a day-zero impact hits Europe and Asia far harder than the US, because the US is exporting its SPR to cushion the world and Gulf exports are overwhelmingly Asia-bound (roughly 90% Asia, ~5% Europe, ~5% other).
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Asian importers (Japan, Korea, India) carry the acute crisis exposure; regional crack spreads and currencies there are the pressure points.
insights.quote: everybody goes, "Oh, there's no energy crisis in Asia." They have They They're in one. Not in the US.

insights.claim: China has throttled back both molecule imports and exports so its net oil position has collapsed, yet oil demand has not fallen at the same rate because it is substituting toward EVs and coal-powered electricity (EV charging up 54% over the May holiday, thermal coal generation up 160% in Q1).
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: China's structural shift to electrons buffers it from the oil crisis and explains resilient demand elsewhere; a key reason the West bears more of the shock.
insights.quote: China has throttled back its molecule exports and imports. So from a net basis it's collapsed. However, demand is unlikely collapse at the same rate.

insights.claim: Satellite data suggests China has not tapped its very large strategic petroleum reserve and is not exporting it, which Currie calls the big mystery of the China oil picture, while he expects China to be the one buyer that returns 'in size' buying physical.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: An untapped Chinese SPR plus expected large physical buying is latent demand that could accelerate prices when it materializes.
insights.quote: that big SPR they haven't tapped it and they're not exporting to the rest of the world

insights.claim: Currie's core thesis is 'revenge of the old economy': commodity capex of ~$400 billion a year is far below the ~$750 billion needed, so reserves and inventories are both depleting and trillions must rotate from the new economy into oil, gas and metals.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Chronic underinvestment is the structural bull case; it implies a multi-year capital rotation trade into the entire commodity complex.
insights.quote: It's running around 400 billion a year. It needs to be up in that 750 range. Reserves are going down.

insights.claim: US oil production has stalled near 13.4-13.5 mb/d and is recently declining because nobody is drilling, with maximum year-over-year growth of only 100,000-150,000 bpd, so domestic supply cannot plug the near-term gap.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: US shale cannot bail out the world quickly; the supply-response ceiling reinforces a tighter-for-longer setup.
insights.quote: it's struggling around 13.5. And by the way, it's been declining more recently cuz nobody's drilling.

insights.claim: Currie notes over half of the increase in US oil exports (from 4 to 6 mb/d) is being supplied straight from the SPR drawing ~1.2 mb/d, meaning the export surge is borrowed from the reserve rather than new production.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Export strength is a reserve-funded illusion that cannot persist past day zero; the apparent supply cushion is temporary.
insights.quote: You're drawing the SPR at over um a million barrels per day. It's somewhere around 1.2 million barrels per day.

insights.claim: Currie expects a capital pendulum swing back to commodities like the 2000-2002 rotation, arguing tech free cash flow yields (even at Google) are likely to go negative as they over-invest, while energy firms enjoy high free cash flow yields and rising return on capital.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A relative-value rotation from tech to energy on free-cash-flow trajectory is the trade; rising ROC favors oil names over tech.
insights.quote: Free cash flow yields are likely even at Google are likely to go negative.

insights.claim: Currie prefers oil companies to crude itself because the equities have dropped 'crazily' back to January levels and erased their 2026 gains, even though crude is only modestly off, making the stocks the cheaper expression of the bull case.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: The dislocation between cheap equities and resilient crude is the actionable entry; buy the companies over the barrel.
insights.quote: the companies have dropped crazily. They're back to like January levels. That's why I like the companies better than the oil right now.

insights.claim: Currie cites Exxon falling from near $170 to around $140 as evidence that even a stable, high-dividend 'grandma stock' has been hammered while its long-term outlook has arguably never been better given Middle East supply and refinery losses.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A blue-chip oil major trading down on improving fundamentals is a concrete value entry point for income-oriented investors.
insights.quote: Exxon was near 170, today it's trading around 140.

insights.claim: Currie argues big-tech AI firms now resemble commodity companies, producing 'AI compute' from data and electrons by putting steel in the ground with heavy capex, yet trade at ~32-40x multiples versus the 10-12x that capital-intensive cyclicals deserve.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: If the market re-rates AI hyperscalers as cyclicals, the multiple compression is severe; a structural short/avoid case for richly-valued tech.
insights.quote: People who put steel in the ground don't get 30-plus multiples. They get like 10 to 12.

insights.claim: Currie says $85 is the new floor needed to incentivize investment and, barring a recession, does not expect $70 or $60 oil at any point this year, directly contradicting the President's repeated claim that a deal would make oil 'drop like a rock'.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: A hard $85 floor view opposes the official jawboning narrative and positions for limited downside absent recession.
insights.quote: No, that's like the new floor. You need 85 there to get any investment.

insights.claim: Currie is aggressively bullish, advising investors to be substantially long oil and associated companies now as a combined income and diversification play, valuing the cash dividends as 'the real yield' with oil's fair value at ~85 even absent the war.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Currie's explicit buy-and-be-aggressive call with cash-yielding majors is the headline actionable stance for the episode.
insights.quote: get long, buckle your seatbelt and hang on for the ride.

insights.claim: Currie names copper as his top non-oil commodity, calling it 'the new oil' and the strategically most important commodity for electrification, noting it has weathered the crisis near 13,600 after a January all-time-high spike to ~14,000.
insights.speaker: Jeff Currie
insights.so_what: Copper as the electrification keystone is the next leg of the commodity rotation; its resilience through the oil shock flags relative strength.
insights.quote: Copper, copper, copper... copper is the king. In fact, I like to say copper is the new oil.
